title: Chicken and waffles
subtitle: Crisp fried chicken thighs on butter waffles with hot sauce and maple syrup
excerpt: Buttermilk-brined fried chicken thighs served on top of freshly cooked waffles, with hot sauce and maple syrup poured over. The combination of savoury, spiced, crispy chicken and sweet, yielding waffle with syrup is one of the more successful odd pairings in American cooking. A substantial meal at any hour.
sourceNotes: Chicken and waffles as a pairing is documented in African American cooking from the mid-twentieth century, associated with Harlem supper clubs such as Wells Restaurant in New York City, which served it as a late-night meal. The combination gained nationwide popularity through Roscoe's House of Chicken and Waffles in Los Angeles (opened 1975) and subsequently through American brunch and soul food restaurant culture.

content.text: This is a dish of contrasts managed deliberately: the chicken must be properly crisp (not just cooked), the waffle must be properly golden (not pale and soft), and the sauces (hot and sweet) must both be on the plate. None of these are optional. Serve the chicken on top of the waffle, pour both the hot sauce and the maple syrup over, and eat immediately before the waffle softens.

The chicken is brined in seasoned buttermilk for at least 4 hours, which tenderises the meat and helps the coating adhere. The coating is seasoned plain flour: paprika, garlic powder, cayenne. Fry in a cast-iron skillet in about 2 cm of oil rather than deep-frying; this gives control over the crust and produces a less greasy result. The oil must be at 175°C before the chicken goes in. Use a thermometer if you have one; otherwise test with a wooden spoon handle; small, steady bubbles indicate the right temperature.
Season the {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk generously with salt and pepper. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs, turn to coat, cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight.

Whisk together 200 g {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and a pinch of salt. In a separate jug, whisk together 250 ml {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk, {{eggs}} eggs, and {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter. Combine wet and dry, stirring until just smooth. Rest for 5 minutes.

Mix the remaining 150 g {{plain-flour}} of plain flour with {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, {{garlic-powder}} of garlic powder, {{cayenne}} of cayenne, salt, and pepper in a wide shallow bowl. Lift the chicken from the buttermilk, letting the excess drip off, then press each piece into the seasoned flour to coat all over. Pour neutral oil to a depth of 2 cm into the cast-iron skillet and heat to 175°C over medium-high heat. Fry the chicken in batches for 8 to 10 minutes per side until the crust is deep golden and the juices run clear when pierced at the thickest point. Drain on a rack.

Preheat the waffle iron and brush with butter. Cook the waffles according to your iron's instructions until golden and crisp, about 4 to 5 minutes each. Place a waffle on each plate, set a piece of fried chicken on top, and drizzle with {{louisiana-hot-sauce}} of hot sauce and {{maple-syrup}} of maple syrup. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Fry in batches and wait for the oil to return to temperature between batches; a thermometer is the most reliable guide
items.cause: Oil not hot enough, or pan overcrowded which drops the temperature
items.symptom: Chicken coating is pale and soft, not crisp

items.fix: Press the chicken firmly into the flour and let it sit for 2 minutes before frying so the coating adheres
items.cause: Flour not pressed firmly onto the surface, or buttermilk not drained sufficiently

items.fix: Fry at a moderate 175°C; if the crust is darkening before the centre is cooked, finish in a 180°C oven for 10 minutes
items.cause: Temperature too high; crust set before the heat reached the bone
items.symptom: Chicken is cooked outside but raw at the bone

items.fix: Cook waffles until fully crisp, not just golden, and serve immediately after assembly
items.cause: Assembled too far in advance or waffle undercooked
items.symptom: Waffle goes soft under the chicken before you eat it

content.text: Chicken and waffles is rooted in the African American cooking tradition of the American South and Northeast. Harlem restaurants in New York served it as a late-night meal in the 1930s and 1940s. Customers came in after a long evening out, so a meal that was both supper and breakfast made sense. Roscoe's House of Chicken and Waffles opened in Los Angeles in 1975. That restaurant pushed the dish to a wider audience and made it a soul food standard. It is now served across American brunch culture as a dish that has avoided being reduced to a gimmick. Perhaps because the pairing itself is hard to argue with.
